title: Stocking-stitch dishcloth
subtitle: The smallest finished knitted object that teaches the whole loop. A 22 by 22 cm cotton dishcloth with a four-stitch garter border.
excerpt: A 22 by 22 cm cotton dishcloth in stocking stitch with a four-stitch garter border. The smallest meaningful finished object: it teaches casting on, knit, purl, stitch counting, casting off, and weaving in ends. Every move scales to bigger projects.
sourceNotes:
Pattern drawn from public-domain references:

• Weldon's Practical Knitting (Victorian, multiple series, 1880s onwards). The cotton dishcloth and washcloth appear across many Weldon's volumes as the standard household first-knit. Internet Archive.
• Beeton's Book of Needlework (1870), §Plain knitting, for the garter-border-on-stocking-stitch construction. Project Gutenberg.

knitting.gaugeText: 22 sts × 30 rows = 10 × 10 cm in stocking stitch on 4 mm needles in DK cotton, lightly steamed.

glossaryTerms.definition: How many stitches and rows fit in a 10 by 10 cm square of finished fabric. Knitters work a small swatch first to check their gauge matches the pattern's, then adjust needle size up or down if not.
The knitted fabric made by knitting one row and purling the next, repeating. Smooth Vs on one side (the right side); horizontal bumps on the other (the wrong side). US name: stockinette.
A few stitches at each edge of a piece worked in garter stitch (knit every row), framing the main stocking-stitch fabric. The garter stitch lies flat where stocking stitch alone would curl.

content.text: A dishcloth is the smallest knitted object that justifies itself. The finished 22 by 22 cm square sits between the sink and the cooker doing real work; it washes plates, wipes down counters, lasts six months of daily use, then goes on the compost. As a first knitted project, it teaches the full loop of knitting in a single weekend: cast on, knit, purl, count stitches, recover from a slip, cast off, weave in the ends. Every single technique transfers to bigger projects.
The pattern is
stocking stitch
(knit one row, purl the next) with a four-stitch
garter border
at each edge to stop the cloth from curling. A garter-only dishcloth is even simpler (knit every row throughout) but doesn't teach the purl stitch, which is half the craft.
What you need

A 50 g ball of DK-weight cotton yarn (about 125 m). Cotton is the right fibre for a dishcloth: it absorbs water, dries fast, and survives a hot wash. Drops Paris, Rico Creative Cotton, or any supermarket cotton DK works. Choose a light colour for the first attempt; the stitches show more clearly while you learn.

A pair of 4 mm knitting needles (UK 8 / US 6) in straight or short circular form. Wooden or bamboo needles grip the cotton better than metal at this stage. One blunt tapestry needle for sewing the ends in. Scissors. A tape measure.
Gauge
The pattern's
gauge

is 22 stitches and 30 rows per 10 cm square in stocking stitch on 4 mm needles. Knit a small 15 cm swatch first if you want the finished cloth to come out exactly 22 by 22 cm. If your swatch comes out smaller than 22 stitches per 10 cm, go down a needle size; if larger, go up. For a first project where ±2 cm doesn't matter, skip the swatch and just start the cloth.
Working the dishcloth
Cast on 48 stitches using the
long-tail cast on
(or any cast-on you know). Then:
Rows 1 to 4: knit every stitch across both sides. This forms the bottom garter border.
Rows 5 to 60 (main body), right side rows: knit 4, knit to last 4 stitches, knit 4 (a full row of knit stitches; the borders sit inside the row already).
Rows 5 to 60 (main body), wrong side rows: knit 4, purl to last 4 stitches, knit 4. The four border stitches at each edge are always knitted; the 40 stitches between are purled.
Rows 61 to 64: knit every stitch across both sides. This forms the top garter border.
Cast off all stitches in knit pattern. Leave a 20 cm tail.

Total: 64 rows. The finished dishcloth measures about 22 cm wide and 21 cm tall before pressing, settling to 22 by 22 cm after a light steam. Thread the tail onto the blunt needle, weave it back and forth through three or four stitches on the back of the work, and trim flush. Do the same with the cast-on tail at the other corner.
Steaming

A light steam settles the fabric to its finished dimensions. Pin the dishcloth out on a towel to 22 by 22 cm square. Hold a steam iron 2 cm above the fabric (don't press it down) and let the steam pass over the whole piece for 30 seconds. Leave to dry in place for an hour. The borders flatten out and the stocking stitch evens up.
Common mistakes

attrs.heading: What goes wrong on a first dishcloth
attrs.intro: Four mistakes that almost every new knitter makes on a first stocking-stitch piece.

items.symptom: Cloth gets wider as it grows
items.cause: Extra stitches picked up at the start of each row, usually by knitting into the slipped first stitch and the loop hanging off the needle as two separate stitches.
items.fix: Count stitches at the end of every row: 48 cast on means 48 at the end of every row. Pull the first stitch firm before knitting it.

items.symptom: Cloth gets narrower as it grows
items.cause: Last stitch of each row slipping off the needle without being worked.
items.fix: Slow down on the last stitch. The yarn pulls firm against the edge as it comes off; that's the cue to check the count.

items.symptom: Cloth curls up at the edges before the borders are finished
items.cause: Stocking stitch always curls; the four-stitch garter border holds it flat once it's set.
items.fix: Once the cloth comes off the needles and the top garter border is done, the curl flattens. A steam at the end settles it for good. If the curl persists, the border may need to be wider (six stitches instead of four) on a future project.

items.symptom: Purl rows look looser than knit rows, leaving visible horizontal stripes (rowing)
items.cause: Most new knitters tension less firmly on purls than on knits, producing a slightly looser purl row.
items.fix: Pull the yarn one tug tighter at the end of each purl stitch. The difference evens out with practice. The steam at the end smooths the small remaining unevenness.

content.text: Where this goes next

A pile of dishcloths is the traditional next step (make six, replace them every couple of months, compost the old ones). After that, the same skills carry to a scarf (longer rectangle, garter or stocking stitch with a wider border), a baby blanket (wider rectangle, multiple colours), or a simple cushion cover (two squares sewn together). The first jumper is still a long way off, but every stitch on the way to it is one of these stitches.
